input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a building operated as a Living Lab that embodies a typical AECO asset. Its core physical components are the architectural structure, integrated lighting systems, and associated sensors and actuators that enable real‑time measurement of illumination, energy consumption, and environmental conditions. The building serves as a testbed for systematic, lifecycle‑spanning digital twinning of lighting infrastructure within the construction‑to‑operation domain.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides simulation, analysis, and optimization services for a cyber‑physical asset (e.g., a building) in the AECO domain. It delivers real‑time insights by continuously ingesting runtime data, supports predictive analytics to forecast future performance, and enables enhanced decision‑making throughout the asset lifecycle, from design through operation and refurbishment.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation relies on a model‑driven deployment framework grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM) for structural and asset representation, Eclipse Hono for secure, scalable IoT data ingestion from lighting sensors, Eclipse Ditto for managing the digital twin’s state and providing a unified data API, and Unreal Engine to render an interactive, photorealistic 3‑D visualization of the building and its lighting systems. Together these tools enable seamless data exchange, digital twin lifecycle management, and rich user interaction.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The DT operates in a real‑time mode, continuously ingesting sensor streams and updating the virtual model as soon as new measurements arrive. Synchronisation between the physical asset and its digital counterpart is performed with low latency to support live monitoring and fast feedback loops, although exact update frequencies are not quantified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows a model‑driven framework that orchestrates a modular, service‑oriented DT platform. Standardised extension points expose reusable services that can be composed to form the full twin, and coordinated roles (e.g., data acquisition, model execution, analytics, and visualisation) are mapped to distinct system components. Resource management is handled centrally within the framework to ensure scalable deployment across heterogeneous infrastructure.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin is built on enriched Building Information Modeling (BIM) files that contain geometric representations of building elements together with semantic extensions for devices. Building Control Engineers embed sensors, actuators and controller definitions directly into the BIM model, creating behavioral models (control logic) and analytical models (performance rules). Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) transformations automatically extract these structured representations from the BIM semantics and generate deployable DT artifacts (runtime interfaces and services). The resulting data flow combines the static BIM geometry with real‑time data streams from the physical devices, establishing explicit relationships between geometric elements, device models and the extracted data, which together form the unified semantic foundation of the twin.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators and control mechanisms are modeled as device entities within the BIM environment by Building Control Engineers. The BIM extensions capture each actuator's identity and link it to the associated control logic that can be invoked remotely through the Digital Twin interface. This enables Facility Managers to command the physical actuators via the twin. The source does not enumerate specific actuator types, actuation ranges, or safety constraints, but the modular architecture provides standardized extension points for integrating custom control services.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are represented as semantic objects in the enriched BIM model. The BIM extensions store sensor configuration, location, and type, allowing the framework to bind each physical sensor to its digital counterpart. Real‑time data exchange capabilities keep sensor measurements continuously synchronized with the twin, supporting Facility Managers in monitoring building states. No detailed specifications such as measurement ranges, sampling frequencies, or accuracy are provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The framework provides real‑time data exchange that continuously synchronises physical building sensor and device readings with their digital counterparts. Operational data generated by building control systems is streamed into the Digital Twin, where it is merged with enriched BIM models to keep the virtual representation up‑to‑date. The document does not specify concrete data types, protocols, frequencies, or preprocessing steps.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Through the same bidirectional data‑exchange hub, the Digital Twin can emit control signals back to the building control systems, enabling Facility Managers to monitor and adjust building operations from the virtual environment. Control commands are sent from the DT to physical actuators or control interfaces, though the document does not detail command formats, validation, or acknowledgment mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework supports the full Digital Twin life‑cycle, starting with device‑modeling within the BIM environment where sensors, actuators and controllers are defined (semantic enrichment of BIM). The enriched BIM model is then processed by Model‑Driven Engineering (MDE) transformations that extract a structured representation (domain model) and automatically generate deployable DT artefacts (runtime services, data‑exchange interfaces). After deployment, a runtime phase provides continuous real‑time data exchange and interactive visualisation for operation monitoring and control. Subsequent phases allow the integration of custom services via standardized extension points, enabling evolutionary updates. Transition between phases is orchestrated automatically by the MDE pipeline, and lifecycle governance is enforced by the functional requirement set (F_X) and the modular architecture that prescribes representation types and hand‑off rules.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: DT creation follows a model‑driven deployment process: Building Control Engineers model devices and control logic in BIM and trigger deployment; MDE transformations convert the enriched BIM into executable DT components; DT Engineers develop and plug‑in custom services via the framework’s standardized extension points, enabling iterative evolution; the modular architecture and runtime configuration support adaptation without manual re‑implementation.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Real‑time data exchange keeps the twin synchronized with the physical building, delivering up‑to‑date state information to Facility Managers; interactive visualization provides an immersive interface for monitoring system states and initiating control actions; this continuous insight supports operational decision making directly through the twin.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The solution addresses the lack of standard integration by defining a modeling methodology that standardises BIM semantics for building control systems and by exposing standardized extension points for custom services. These extension points follow a common interface specification, enabling interoperability between the DT core and third‑party analytics or control plugins. The framework’s modular architecture and functional requirement set (F_X) provide a compliance framework that enforces consistent use of the standardised BIM extensions across the deployment lifecycle.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the Living Lab as a typical AECO asset with architectural structure, integrated lighting systems, sensors, and actuators. It correctly identifies its role as a testbed for digital twinning in the construction-to-operation domain. This is well-supported by evidence in the abstract and introduction, particularly the mention of a 'Living Lab case study' and the context provided by TwinLight and BOREALIS projects focusing on lighting infrastructure. However, the specific details about 'real-time measurement of illumination, energy consumption, and environmental conditions' are implied by the general description of the projects but are not explicitly detailed in the provided text snippets. Thus, the score is 4 because it is correct and specific but could be more grounded in the explicit text provided.

The description is highly accurate and well-grounded in the source text. It correctly lists the core services—simulation, analysis, optimization—and the capabilities such as real-time insights, predictive analytics, and decision-making across the lifecycle. The text directly supports these points. Quotes: 'virtual representation... for simulation, analysis, and optimization' and 'providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities'.

The extraction correctly identifies Building Information Modeling (BIM) as the backbone, Eclipse Hono for IoT data ingestion, Eclipse Ditto for state management and API, and Unreal Engine for 3-D visualization. This is strongly supported by multiple quotes from the source documents, such as 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM),' 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange,' and 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization.' The description is highly specific and well-grounded in the provided sources, justifying a score of 5.

I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the real-time nature of the DT, supported by mentions of 'real-time monitoring' and 'live synchronization' in the text. The claim that exact frequencies are not quantified is correct based on the provided text. The extraction is specific but lacks deeper technical detail on latency values present in similar engineering specs, fitting a 'Good' rating. Supported by quotes: 'enabling real-time monitoring', 'continuous synchronization with physical assets', 'live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', and the source documents do not contain information regarding multiple digital twin instances, hierarchical structures, or coordination mechanisms between twins for the proposed system. The text focuses on generating a DT from a BIM model rather than managing multiple instances or their orchestration.

The extraction provides a highly specific and detailed description of the system's orchestration, correctly identifying it as a 'model-driven framework' with 'standardized extension points' and a 'central hub'. It accurately captures the coordinated roles and modular, service-oriented nature described in the sources. Supported by quotes: 'model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment', 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms', 'coordinated roles and system components', 'DT Framework acts as a central hub'.

The source text discusses internal components like BIM, Eclipse Hono, Ditto, and Unreal Engine, and mentions 'interoperability across heterogeneous systems' as a requirement. However, it does not describe specific integration patterns, data exchange protocols, or dependencies on external enterprise systems or other Digital Twins as defined by the characteristic.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the model-driven framework that uses enriched BIM files to combine geometric representations with semantic extensions. It correctly identifies the generation of deployable DT artifacts via MDE transformations and the integration of real-time data streams (Hono/Ditto) to establish a unified semantic foundation. This matches the source's detailed description of the framework's architecture and data flow.

The extraction correctly states that actuators are modeled as device entities within the BIM environment by Building Control Engineers and are linked to control logic for remote invocation by Facility Managers. It also accurately notes that specific actuator types and ranges are not listed in the source, while correctly identifying the modular architecture with extension points for custom services.

This characteristic is well-extracted, describing sensors as semantic objects within the BIM model that store configuration and location data. It accurately captures the real-time data exchange capabilities (via Hono/Ditto) for synchronization and correctly identifies the absence of detailed technical specifications like measurement ranges or sampling frequencies in the provided text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text actually mentions specific validation and fidelity considerations. Specifically, it describes the use of the 'Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric' to measure how well the DT reflects its physical counterpart (verification) and discusses 'semantic drift' and 'synchronization techniques' to ensure integrity (error detection and validity). Therefore, evidence does exist.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

The source text mentions the integration of 'Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange' and the extraction of metadata such as 'tenant and device ID or endpoint addresses'. However, it does not describe specific cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, threat mitigation strategies, fail-safe mechanisms, or safety-critical system considerations.
